title: Measuring the complete set of spatial Schmidt modes of entangled two-photon fields
authors: Radhika Prasad, Nilakshi Senapati, Abhinandan Bhattacharjee, Suman Karan, Anand K. Jha
abstract: Spontaneous parametric down-conversion (SPDC) is the most widely-used source of high-dimensional entangled two-photon states, and the entanglement in the spatial degree of freedom is considered best suited for harnessing high-dimensional advantages. Although the Schmidt basis provides a natural choice for state characterisation of entangled two-photon states in any degree of freedom, there is currently no technique that can measure the Schmidt basis of an entangled two-photon field. The existing techniques can only reconstruct the Schmidt spectrum when the Schmidt basis is known a priori. In contrast, we present a technique that measures the complete set of spatial Schmidt modes without any prior knowledge. Using this technique, we report measurement of states with over 3000 Schmidt modes -- highest reported yet -- with up to 98$\%$ fidelity. We expect our work to significantly advance the harnessing of high-dimensional advantages in SPDC-based systems.
summary: The researchers have demonstrated a new way to identify the spatial patterns (Schmidt modes) of entangled photon pairs without knowing them beforehand. By measuring the coherence of a single photon, they successfully mapped out over 3000 modes with high accuracy. This breakthrough enables much easier use of high-dimensional light for complex quantum communication and computing tasks.
main_problem: Existing techniques for characterizing high-dimensional entangled two-photon states can only reconstruct the Schmidt spectrum if the Schmidt basis is known a priori, and often suffer from low efficiency or long data collection times.
main_result: The authors developed a technique to measure the complete set of spatial Schmidt modes without prior knowledge, achieving measurement of states with over 3000 Schmidt modes with up to 98% fidelity.
method: The method uses a Sagnac-type interferometer with a 4f lens configuration to measure the cross-spectral density function (CSDF) of a single photon, which is then numerically diagonalized to extract the Schmidt modes and spectrum.
model_or_system: The system consists of high-dimensional entangled two-photon states generated via type-I, collinear, degenerate Spontaneous Parampectrum Down-Conversion (SPDC) using a UV laser and a BBO crystal.
key_observables: Detection probability, cross-spectral density function (CSDF), degree of spatial coherence, Schmidt spectrum (eigenvalues), and Schmidt modes (eigenfunctions).
important_parameters: Pump beam waist (507 um), crystal thickness (1 mm), magnification factor (m=2 and 1/2), and Schmidt number (approx. 3413).
main_assumptions: The signal photon field is assumed to be quasi-homogeneous, meaning the degree of coherence depends only on the difference in coordinates.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the conceptual measurement process; Figure 2 shows the experimental setup; Figure 3 displays recorded interferograms and reconstructed coherence/CSDF; Figure 4 shows the reconstructed Schmidt spectrum; Figure 5 displays reconstructed spatial Schmidt modes up to order 50.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of basis-dependent measurement, describes the experimental setup and interferometer-based reconstruction method, presents the results for high-dimensional mode measurement and fidelity, and discusses limitations related to camera resolution.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and photonic quantum information as it provides a way to characterize and potentially manipulate extremely high-dimensional qudits without needing prior knowledge of their spatial structure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Conceptual illustration of the scheme. One of the SPDC photons (say, the signal photon) goes through an interferometer which measures its cross-spectral density function W(ρs, ρ′ s), which is numerically diagonalized to yield the eigenfunctions ϕmn(ρs) and the corresponding eigenvalues λmn, which are also the Schmidt modes and Schmidt spectrum of the entangled two-photon state ψsi (ρs, ρi).

FIG. 2. Experimental setup. LP: Longpass filter to transmit HeNe and reflect UV; BBO: β-Barium Borate crystal; DM: Dichroic mirror to transmit SPDC photons and block UV; BS: Beam Splitter; HWP: Half-Wave Plate; BP: Bandpass filter of bandwidth 10 nm centered at 710 nm. HeNe laser is used only for aligning the interferometer and is turned off when UV laser is used. We represent the interfering alternatives with different shades of dark red for the beam reflected at the BS, and light red for the beam transmitted at the BS.

FIG. 3. Experimental observations and reconstructions. (a), (b), (c) are recorded interferograms Iϕ=π/4 out (xs, ys), Iϕ=0 out (xs, ys), and their difference. (d) and (e) are the intensities profiles corresponding to the magnified and demagnified alternatives. (f) is the reconstructed degree of coherence function µ(x, x′). (g) and (h) are the reconstructed Wx(x, x′) and Wy(y, y′).
FIG. 4. Reconstructed Schmidt spectrum λmn. F represents the fidelity of reconstruction.
FIG. 5. Plots of experimentally reconstructed Schmidt modes ϕmn(x, y). F is the fidelity of reconstruction.
